body:
### Problem

(This is a "work plan" for https://github.com/neovim/neovim/issues/22313 )

https://github.com/nvim-treesitter/nvim-treesitter should be upstreamed in a form similar to [nvim-lspconfig](https://github.com/neovim/nvim-lspconfig), using the power of magic 🌈 

Known issues:

- queries may be editor specific (nvim vs helix vs ...)
- parsers don't always maintain their own queries
- supply-chain attacks (malicious parsers)

### Expected behavior

Users can easily install the "top 20" treesitter parsers with a single `vim.pack.add{}` call. (Note: Nvim already ships 5 builtin parsers.)

- Provide a ~~`neovim/nvim-tsconfig`~~ `neovim/nvim-treeconfig` repo which has a similar role as `neovim/nvim-lspconfig`.
    - It is "data-only", it doesn't have a "framework". No "indent" support. (Features requiring a framework should live in Nvim's own stdlib.)
    - Limited scope, only support the "top N" parsers. (Can reconsider this if/when we find a sustainble maintenance model.)
    - Parser-related shit (including queries) is owned by the parsers.
- See sub-issues.

### Reference

Other nvim-treesitter alternatives:

- https://github.com/lewis6991/ts-install.nvim
- https://github.com/arborist-ts/arborist.nvim
- https://github.com/romus204/tree-sitter-manager.nvim

comment_data.body:
@justinmk Just a thought on the name `nvim-tsconfig`: "tsconfig" is very likely going to get confused with [TypeScript's tsconfig.json](https://www.typescriptlang.org/tsconfig/).

While it's not as consistent with the naming of nvim-lspconfig, I would like to suggest the name "treesitter-config.nvim". Thoughts?

It's really not a big deal, because "ts" in the context of *Nvim core concepts* never means "typescript". 

Anyway no point in debating, this is all vaporware currently.

> I would like to suggest the name "treesitter-config.nvim".

That is randomly different than the `nvim-lspconfig` naming convention which was chosen quite carefully. 

~~We probably should just name it... `nvim-treesitter`.~~ No, that's too confusing.

[Arborist.nvim](https://github.com/arborist-ts/arborist.nvim) guy here. 

I started this as an experiment. I've been daily driving it. And daily bug fixing it. Anyway...

I'm happy to hand over the Arborist org to anyone who wants to maintain it. 

And happy to deprecate it if the community chooses to consolidate around one direction. 

No pride in owning it here. But glad to chip in as a community member. 

I just need a Treesitter manager I can install and the promptly forget about.

Any plans for a binary only distribution method? Or perhaps build in a container if podman or docker are in path? I don't think it is at all reasonable one needs to have a full C build toolchain available to add TS parsers to enable decent highlighting for languages neovim has _chosen_ to not ship support for. Not all of us partook in nvim-treesitter but a simple copypasta from X to Y seems like discarding the baby and the bathwater (understood that isn't entirely what is stated here but I am reading between the lines).

Note: Nvim already shipping 5 builtin parsers isn't enough. Why not ship 50+? Other editors include support for highlighting dozens of languages ootb. Why do we still need yet another plugin to achieve basic usability?

> I don't think it is at all reasonable one needs to have a full C build toolchain

@jmpolom you did not read the proposal here (and sub-issues). I'm going to start deleting low-effort comments without explanation. And maybe just ban anyone that wastes my time like this.

> Other editors include support for highlighting dozens of languages ootb. Why do we still need yet another plugin to achieve basic usability?

Vim/Nvim ships syntax highlighting for 700+ languages.

> @jmpolom you did not read the proposal here (and sub-issues). I'm going to start deleting low-effort comments without explanation. And maybe just ban anyone that wastes my time like this.

Wow. And I've read enough. I mean I use the tool, what more do you want.

> Vim/Nvim ships syntax highlighting for 700+ languages.

TS is the way forward?

https://github.com/neovim/neovim/issues/22313#issuecomment-1435703489 mentioned coordination with other editors. Has there been any attempt at doing that? If not is that an avenue worth pursuing? 

It would simplify things quite a bit if upstream treesitter projects could include their own queries that would work for most editors that use treesitter instead of each needing its own queries.

>  Has there been any attempt at doing that?

Yes, intensely. You can see the results (and limits) of that. (Spoiler: Zed doesn't even use the same capture names as upstream tree-sitter.)

> coordination with other editors. Has there been any attempt at doing that?

- https://github.com/neovim/neovim/pull/22518

Does this mean that when it gets built into Neovim, third-party parsers won't be allowed? I really don't want to see all this effort be put toward making it a builtin thing, only to require a plugin in order to install "less popular" parsers. 

I really don't think there should be a popularity contest on which language is more important to support. It's like saying that hammer is more popular than a screwdriver, and so we only support the hammer, even though the screwdriver is important to one's work as well. Parsers (besides the core 5 ones already builtin) should have a way to be easily installed and managed by the user, focus more on making an easy way to import other people's parsers instead of shipping the coolest 20 you choose that may not even be used by me or someone else.

One of the comments above that was marked as low-quality (for some reason) had a good point on binary parsers. It really shouldn't be *required* to have a toolchain to install parsers, but that's a different issue altogether. I don't like when people delete/hide things without offering an explanation on why something is a bad idea. If nvim-treesitter becomes a bad plugin, I'll make a fork and live my way, but this is making it into the core Neovim, it should be discussed to the fullest extent possible, otherwise you'd be making decisions based on speculation and personal preference.

@alexmozaidze this is not about anything "builtin", where did you get that from? This is about creating and maintaining a repo similar to `nvim-treesitter`.

Please read the description and the sub-issues, and avoid skipping to wild random conclusions.

> One of the comments above that was marked as low-quality (for some reason) had a good point on binary parsers

Because that is literally addressed in the sub-issue https://github.com/neovim/neovim/issues/39007 . 🤦 

> I don't like when people delete/hide things without offering an explanation on why something is a bad idea. If nvim-treesitter becomes a bad plugin, I'll make a fork and live my way, but this is making it into the core Neovim, it should be discussed to the fullest extent possible, otherwise you'd be making decisions based on speculation and personal preference.

We have already discussed it to death, and there are even references to previous discussions given here. So drive-by comments adding nothing are not welcome.

One important aspect of making it only "top 20" languages is at least taking into account how people are meant to distribute other languages. Properly documenting and making it as easy as possible would probably go a long way.

Let's say someone wants to add tree-sitter support for vim9script. What are the recommended steps to do that? My understanding based on the current suggestions:

- Publish a 'tree-sitter-vim9script' repo that contains both parser logic and queries. Suggest to install it with `vim.pack.add()` directly? But then queries are in the form of 'queries/*.scm' and not 'queries/vim9script/*.scm'.

- Add a CI that builds a WASM parser. Then suggest users to manually download and install it?

I think realistically at least some Neovim-specific layer of queries and/or installer would be needed. So it is an extra:

- Publish a 'vim9script.nvim' plugin that contains 'queries/vim9script/*.scm' runtime files. Maybe more ('ftplugin/', 'lsp/', etc.). Suggest to `vim.pack.add()` it instead of a raw parser.

- Inside that plugin by its maintainer document that the queries are written for `https://github.com/vim-user/tree-sitter-vim9script` parser at revision 'abcdef123'. In a way that can be reused by a "tree-sitter installer plugin" (be it maybe 'nvim-treeconfig' itself or not).

    For this using an existing convention of `:h treesitter-query-modeline` seems like a least friction approach. Add a `;; parser https://github.com/vim-user/tree-sitter-vim9script abcdef123` on top of the query file and it is readable for any plugin. It is then a matter of a "tree-sitter installer plugin" to properly make specified version of this parser available to the user.

@justinmk, do you think this needs/deserves a dedicated issue?

@echasnovski yes feel free to create a subissue for that. But also just to note, the "top 20" (or "top X") thing is to limit scope initially. If/when we find a sustainable approach we can accept *le deluge* similar to nvim-lspconfig.

Pushing queries into the parse repositories is a good idea, but this does pose a question: what should the structure for the queries directory be, and how would that work if the parser wants to support multiple languages?

As an example, imagine https://github.com/inko-lang/tree-sitter-inko/ wants to support both Helix and Neovim, and both move to a model of pushing the queries into the parser repositories. Both these editors use different capture groups and possibly even different queries for e.g. indents. The result is that dumping queries in `queries/LANG/QUERY.scm` (e.g. `queries/inko/indents.scm`) won't work as there's no mechanism for grouping them per editor/consumer.

The structure `queries/CONSUMER/LANG/QUERY.scm` (e.g. `queries/nvim/inko/indents.scm`) wouldn't really work because the structure of `queries/nvim` isn't something you can directly add to the runtime path as it currently would expect something like `queries/nvim/queries/inko/indents.scm`.

The structure `CONSUMER/queries/LANG/QUERY.scm` (e.g. `nvim/queries/inko/indents.scm`) would be better and make it easier to add the directories to the runtime path, effectively only needing something like this:

```
for dir in parser-directories
  runtime_path += dir/nvim
```

> It is "data-only", it doesn't have a "framework". No "indent" support. (Features requiring a framework should live in Nvim's own stdlib.)

So just so I understand this correctly, does this mean that indent support is to be moved in nvim itself, or is indent support going to be removed entirely?

> what should the structure for the queries directory be ... 

that's good info to mention in https://github.com/neovim/neovim/issues/39037


> So just so I understand this correctly, does this mean that indent support is to be moved in nvim itself, or is indent support going to be removed entirely?

if a mature indent approach is found then we will upstream it. it's a low priority for now though. nothing is "removed" since none of this exists yet.

I added some comments to that thread ( https://github.com/neovim/neovim/issues/39037#issuecomment-4246285766 ) but to reiterate willingness to help here:

treestitter already has WASM compile reusable workflows.  I've requested they add swap space controls (PR here https://github.com/tree-sitter/workflows/pull/64 ) which will help those be successful for larger grammars in GitHub CI/CD action contexts.

I have some additional CI/CD workflows which might be helpful in ensuring queries and parsers match, helping maintain separate concerns of installer vs query-parser pairings.  If there's interest in those, or kicking off a discussion of what the reasoning is behind design decisions on that sketch (particularly the need for inheritance / injection support is tricky), then i'd be happy to help make something sustainable - even if it's not that sketch which ends up being the implementation

> So just so I understand this correctly, does this mean that indent support is to be moved in nvim itself,

See https://github.com/neovim/neovim/issues/38818
